ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Economic Rice (Cai Fan). FOODON_03315595 (cooked rice dish) is the closest defensible parent class. A more specific term for mixed-topping rice dishes is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Rice-based composite dish — cooked white rice with multiple meat toppings; mapped to FoodEx2 composite dish category for rice-based preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Singaporean Cai Fan / Economic Rice. A00LC (composite dish, cereal-based) is the closest defensible anchor. Annotated as closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept maps to Economic Rice as a specific food entity. SNOMED CT is not applicable for this record.

main_ingredients: mixed meat dishes (pork, chicken, or other meats)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Economic Rice with 3 meat dishes typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of steamed white rice and three portions of meat dishes each averaging 65–70 g.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (3 meats) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (3 meats), CR ID F05100045. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken verbatim from this record. Saturated fat is not reported by HPB for this entry and is set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — White rice, cooked (Jasmine/long-grain, Southeast Asian preparation)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean Economic Rice (3 meats) as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 72 is assigned based on the GI of cooked white rice (jasmine/long-grain, Southeast Asian preparation) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2021), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The three meat components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 26.6) / 100 ≈ 19.1, based on a 400 g serving providing approximately 26.6 g available carbohydrate (16.49 g/100 g × 400 g / 100 × 0.4 rice fraction adjustment). Input record GL of 11.9 appears to be calculated on a smaller portion basis; this record uses the standard 400 g plate serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 250 mg/100 g with low potassium and low dietary fibre offset
hypertension.guidance: A standard 400 g plate delivers approximately 1000 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO daily limit of 2000 mg — and the absence of a vegetable dish means potassium intake is insufficient to counterbalance sodium's pressor effect; patients with hypertension should request reduced soy and oyster sauce, add at least one vegetable dish, and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 72) white rice with low dietary fibre (0.98 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The high-GI white rice base (GI 72) combined with only 0.98 g/100 g dietary fibre produces a high glycemic load per serving (~19), causing rapid postprandial glucose excursions; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce rice portion to half, substitute brown or parboiled rice where available, and add a vegetable dish to slow gastric emptying and blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and likely saturated fat from three meat dishes without vegetable component
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Three meat dishes without vegetables provide a sodium load of ~1000 mg per plate and likely contribute significant saturated fat (saturated fat not reported by HPB but expected to be elevated given mixed meat preparations with soy and oyster sauce); individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose lean meat options such as steamed chicken or fish, request sauce on the side, and add a vegetable dish to improve the dietary fibre and potassium profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (3 Meats), known in Mandarin as 经济饭 (Jīng Jì Fàn) and in Malay as Nasi Ekonomi, is a quintessential Singaporean hawker-centre meal consisting of a generous serving of steamed white jasmine rice accompanied by three self-selected meat dishes chosen from a display of pre-cooked options. Typical meat choices include braised pork belly, char siu (barbecued pork), lemon chicken, fried fish, stewed tofu with minced pork, or egg dishes, all prepared with soy sauce, oyster sauce, and cooking oil. The dish is assembled to order at the cai fan (菜饭) stall and served on a single plate or tray. Per 100 g, it provides 192 kcal, 11.77 g protein, 8.76 g fat, 16.49 g carbohydrate, 1.61 g sugar, 0.98 g dietary fibre, and 250 mg sodium, reflecting the combined contribution of rice and three meat portions. The high protein content (11.77 g/100 g) is a nutritional strength, but the high sodium (250 mg/100 g, approximately 1000 mg per plate), high-GI white rice base (GI ~72), and low dietary fibre make this meal a concern for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The absence of a vegetable dish in the three-meat configuration further reduces potassium, antioxidants, and fibre intake. Clinically, adding a vegetable dish, reducing rice portion, and requesting less sauce are the primary dietary modifications recommended. This dish is a staple working-lunch option across Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops, priced affordably and consumed daily by a broad cross-section of the population.
common_user_queries: How many calories in economic rice 3 meats?
Is economic rice suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat economic rice with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05100045), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The match is exact. GI is estimated from the closest analogous approved GI source (University of Sydney GI Database, white rice, Southeast Asian preparation) and is well-supported by the literature. Confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; GI is an analogous estimate with documented basis.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these fields are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for Economic Rice (3 meats) (F05100045) as of April 2026. Detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish. 2. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on cooked white rice (jasmine/long-grain, Southeast Asian preparation) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Economic Rice (3 meats) as a composite dish exists in any approved GI source. 3. Glycemic load per serving recalculated at 19.1 based on a 400 g standard plate serving; the input record GL of 11.9 appears to reflect a smaller portion. 4. Halal status is marked false because the standard cai fan offering includes pork dishes; halal-certified variants exist but are not the default. 5. Calcium reported as 4.0 mg/100 g and iron as 0.08 mg/100 g per HPB source — these are low values consistent with a rice-dominant dish with limited vegetable content.
